title: Why physical illness often follows major emotional breakthroughs
author: Kalesh

The crash after the climb 

 I remember the week I finally told my therapist the truth about what happened when I was seven. I walked out of her office fe...
htmlContent:
The crash after the climb

I remember the week I finally told my therapist the truth about what happened when I was seven. I walked out of her office feeling lighter than I had in decades. Like someone had lifted a concrete slab off my chest. I called my partner, laughing and crying at the same time. I thought, this is it. This is the breakthrough I've been waiting for.

Three days later I was in bed with a fever of 102. My joints ached. My throat felt like sandpaper. I couldn't keep food down. And I sat there, shivering under three blankets, thinking: what the hell is wrong with me? I finally do the hard emotional work, and my body retaliates?

Here's the thing. That wasn't retaliation. That was my body finally feeling safe enough to tell the truth.

I've seen this pattern play out in my own life and in the lives of dozens of people I've worked with. You have a major emotional breakthrough. You cry tears you've been holding for years. You set a boundary with a parent. You finally admit you were abused. And then, like clockwork, you get sick. A cold. A flu. A migraine that lasts three days. Your chronic pain flares up. Your digestion goes haywire.

And you think you're broken. Or that the healing isn't working. Or that you somehow made things worse.

You didn't. Stay with me here, because this is where the science gets wild.

What the nervous system actually does during emotional release

Stephen Porges, the neuroscientist who developed Polyvagal Theory, spent decades mapping how our nervous system responds to threat and safety. His work shows that when we're in survival mode, our body prioritizes immediate danger over long-term health. Digestion slows down. Immune function gets suppressed. Inflammation is dampened. All so you can run from the tiger.

But here's what most people don't realize. When you finally feel safe, when you have that emotional breakthrough and your nervous system shifts from protection to connection, your body doesn't just relax. It starts doing the maintenance it put off for years. It starts clearing out the backlog.

Think about it. If you've been in a state of chronic hypervigilance, your immune system was running on emergency power. It was doing just enough to keep you alive. When you finally drop into a ventral vagal state, when your body trusts that the danger has passed, it kicks into cleanup mode. It starts addressing the inflammation. It starts processing the cellular debris. It starts doing the deep repair work that got postponed.

That repair work looks a lot like being sick.

Look, I know this is heavy material. And I know there's a part of you that might be thinking "this is too much" or "I can't handle this" or "maybe I should just close this tab." That part is trying to protect you. And that's okay. But I want you to know that you can handle this. You've already handled worse. The fact that you're here, reading this, means your survival system did its job. You made it.

What we're doing here isn't about reliving the past. It's about understanding it well enough to stop being controlled by it. There's a difference between being trapped in a memory and looking at it from a safe distance. You get to choose how close you get. You get to choose when to step back. That choice - that agency - is itself a form of healing.

Peter Levine, the developer of Somatic Experiencing, calls this the "pendulation" process. The nervous system moves between activation and discharge. Between tension and release. And the release phase can feel awful. It can feel like the flu. It can feel like exhaustion. It can feel like your body is falling apart.

Because in a way, it is. The old protective patterns are falling apart. The armor is cracking. And that's messy work.

I've had clients tell me they felt like they were "getting worse" after a breakthrough session. They'd have panic attacks that felt more intense than before. They'd have nightmares. Their chronic pain would spike. And every time, I'd tell them the same thing: your nervous system is finally letting go. The symptoms aren't a sign of failure. They're a sign that your body trusts you enough to release what it's been holding.

The immune system's delayed response to safety

There's a concept in trauma research called the "window of tolerance," which Dan Siegel, a clinical professor of psychiatry at UCLA, developed. It describes the zone where you can process experiences without becoming overwhelmed or shutting down. When you're outside that window, you're either hyperaroused (panic, rage, hypervigilance) or hypoaroused (numb, collapsed, dissociated).

Here's what happens with emotional breakthroughs. You expand your window of tolerance. You integrate something that was previously too overwhelming to process. And in that expansion, your body gets a signal: okay, we're safe enough to feel this now.

But that "feeling" isn't just emotional. It's physiological. Your immune system, which was previously suppressed to keep you functional, now ramps up. It starts attacking latent viruses. It starts addressing low-grade infections. It starts producing cytokines, the inflammatory markers that make you feel tired and achy and feverish.

This is why people often get sick after periods of intense emotional release. It's not a coincidence. It's your immune system finally doing its job.

I've read case studies of people who developed autoimmune flare-ups after trauma therapy. I've seen people develop shingles, of all things, after a breakthrough. Shingles is the reactivation of the chickenpox virus, which lives dormant in your nerve tissue. When your immune system gets busy clearing emotional debris, it sometimes lets the virus slip through.

And that's terrifying, right? You do the work to heal, and your body attacks itself.

But here's the reframe. Your body isn't attacking you. It's finally strong enough to fight. It's finally resourced enough to deal with what it's been holding. The flare-up isn't the problem. The suppression was the problem.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link). It walks through exactly how to stay regulated during these release phases.

Why we blame ourselves for getting sick after breakthroughs

I think part of the problem is that we've been sold a fantasy about healing. The wellness industry wants you to believe that if you do the right breathwork, take the right supplements, say the right affirmations, you'll feel amazing all the time. That healing is an upward trajectory. That breakthroughs feel like sunshine and rainbows.

That's toxic positivity dressed up as spirituality. And it's dangerous.

Real healing is messy. It's nonlinear. It often looks like getting worse before you get better. Because your body has to pass through the storm to get to the calm. It has to discharge the stored energy from old traumas. And that discharge can look like illness, exhaustion, pain, and emotional volatility.

I've had to learn this the hard way, over and over. Every time I have a major emotional insight, I brace myself for the physical crash. I know that my body will need extra rest, extra warmth, extra gentleness. I know that I'll probably get a cold or feel like I'm coming down with something. And I've stopped fighting it.

Now, when I feel that familiar ache creeping in after a therapy session, I don't panic. I don't think I'm broken. I think: okay, my body is doing its cleanup work. I need to support it.

A tool that many find helpful for releasing stored body tension is TriggerPoint GRID Foam Roller (paid link). Using it gently on your back and hips can help move some of that physical charge that gets released during emotional work.

Your body remembers what your mind has forgotten. That's not poetry - that's neuroscience. Peter Levine spent decades documenting how trauma lives in the tissues, in the fascia, in the way your muscles hold tension patterns that mirror the exact posture you were in when the worst thing happened.

I've seen this in my own body. The way my shoulders round forward when I feel threatened - that's the fetal position, compressed into a standing posture. The way my jaw clenches before I even know I'm stressed - that's years of swallowed words, swallowed screams, swallowed truth. Your body is an archive. It stores everything.

Here's what I want you to try. Put one hand on your chest and one on your belly. Don't try to change anything. Just notice. Is your breath high and shallow? Is your belly tight? Are your shoulders up by your ears? Whatever you find, it's okay. You're not doing it wrong. You're just meeting yourself where you are. That's the beginning of everything.

A breathing tool that helps slow the exhale for nervous system regulation is Breathing Exercise Tool (paid link). It's simple but effective when you're in that post-breakthrough crash and need to signal safety to your system.

How to actually the post-breakthrough crash

So what do you do when you have a breakthrough and then your body falls apart?

First, stop trying to push through it. This is the hardest thing for most of us. We're conditioned to believe that productivity equals worth. That rest is weakness. That we should be able to have a breakthrough and then immediately go back to work, back to caregiving, back to performing.

You can't. Your body is doing heavy labor. It's processing decades of stored survival energy. That's real work. And it requires real rest.

Second, hydrate. I know this sounds basic, but emotional release is physically dehydrating. Tears contain stress hormones. When you cry, you're literally excreting cortisol. Your body needs water to flush out the metabolic byproducts of that release.

Third, support your nervous system with gentle regulation. Slow exhales. Warm baths. Gentle movement. Lying on the floor with your legs up the wall. Whatever signals safety to your specific system.

One supplement that many people find calming for their nervous system is Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate (paid link). Magnesium supports the parasympathetic nervous system and can help with the muscle tension and sleep disturbances that often accompany post-breakdown crashes.

Fourth, and this is the hardest one: don't pathologize the crash. Don't tell yourself that you're broken, that the healing isn't working, that you're somehow doing it wrong. The crash is part of the process. It's not a detour. It's the road.

I've had to learn to say to myself: "My body is clearing. This discomfort is temporary. I am safe." And then I let myself rest without guilt.

Fifth, consider working with a therapist who understands the somatic dimension of trauma. Janina Fisher, who wrote "Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors," talks about how the body holds different parts of us that carry different burdens. When one part releases, another part might surface. And that surfacing can feel like illness.

A therapist who knows this terrain won't panic when you get sick after a breakthrough. They'll normalize it. They'll help you resource. They'll remind you that this is part of the process.

The open ending

I'm not going to tell you that the post-breakthrough crash gets easier. It doesn't, exactly. But your relationship to it can change. You can stop fearing it. You can stop interpreting it as a sign that something is wrong.

You can learn to trust your body's timing. To trust that when it asks for rest, it's because rest is what's needed. To trust that the fever, the ache, the exhaustion are not punishments. They're your body finally, finally, finally letting go.

And that's worth getting sick over, honestly. It's worth the crash. Because on the other side of it, there's more freedom. More aliveness. More capacity to actually be here, in your body, without the armor.

The armor kept you safe. But it also kept you sick. And when it cracks, when it falls away, your body gets to do what it's been wanting to do all along: heal.

Even if that healing looks like a fever for three days.

Even if it looks like crying on the bathroom floor.

Even if it looks like nothing at all from the outside, while your insides are rearranging themselves into something that can finally hold the weight of being fully alive.

That's not brokenness. That's the work. And you're doing it.
